data.title: SEC moves to formally rescind corporate climate disclosure requirements
data.description:
The Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) formally proposed Friday to rescind its 2024 rule that would have required publicly traded companies to disclose certain information related to climate change.

The SEC, which made up of three Republican commissioners and no Democrats, issued a statement outlining its plans to scrap the rule.

The rule in question, which passed when the commission had three Democrats and two Republicans, would have required all publicly traded companies to tell investors about ways in which climate change poses significant risks to their business.

It would have also required large companies to disclose information about greenhouse gas emissions directly caused by their business if that information would be likely to influence someone’s decision on whether to invest.

At the time, then-chair Gary Gensler said the move would give investors the ability to clearly compare companies’ contributions to and risks from climate change, which worsens extreme weather.

However, the SEC now describes the rule as going beyond the scope of its authority. It also described the rule as unnecessary, going beyond the policy concerns of securities laws and imposing significant costs on publicly traded companies. 

“SEC disclosure obligations should comply with the Commission’s statutory authority, be guided by materiality as the North Star, avoid the practical effect of dictating corporate behavior, and be imposed only when the expected benefits justify the likely costs and burdens,” SEC Chair Paul Atkins said in a statement.

The move is not a surprise. Last year, the SEC voted to stop defending the 2024 rule against court challenges. 

Since that time, the litigation has been put on pause while the commission reconsiders the rule.

data.title: Bolton slams proposed Iran deal: 'Big defeat for the United States'
data.description:
Former national security adviser John Bolton sharply criticized the Trump administration’s approach to negotiating with Iran, calling a tentative deal under consideration a “mistake” driven by President Trump’s concerns about high gas prices ahead of the midterm elections.

“It’s a big defeat for the United States,” Bolton, who served in Trump’s first term, said in an interview Thursday on NewsNation’s “Elizabeth Vargas Reports.”

“I think this is a mistake. I think it is being undertaken not because of any strategic analysis of the importance to the United States. It’s because of the administration’s fear of high gasoline prices at the pump in the United States,” he added. “That is what is driving this.”

The tentative deal, first reported by Axios on Thursday, would be a 60-day memorandum of understanding (MOU) to extend the fragile ceasefire and reopen the Strait of Hormuz, which would begin to relieve the enormous economic pressure facing both Washington and Tehran.  

Iran would agree not to impose tolls on commercial ships trying to cross the key waterway, through which more than 20 percent of the world’s oil and gas supply passes, and to remove all mines from the strait.  

In exchange, the U.S. would lift its naval blockade and issue some sanctions waivers so Iran could freely sell its oil. The U.S. would also commit to discussing broader sanctions relief.

The MOU would also include a commitment from Iran to not pursue a nuclear weapon, but Tehran has for years made similar assertions that have been disputed by nonproliferation experts. The first issues up for negotiation would be how to dispose of Iran’s highly enriched uranium and how to address future enrichment, Axios reported. 

Bolton, who also previously served as U.S. ambassador to the United Nations, said he worries Iran could use the agreement to buy time to rebuild its capabilities while negotiations continue.

“What Iran is counting on is that, as this process drags out — as all the key issues are kicked down the road — that our forces will begin to melt away, and that they will be able to sell oil, to replenish their assets, rebuild their military program, and take away the biggest threat to the regime that we have, which is the economic threat,” he said.

Bolton said he thinks the chances of Trump following through on threats to resume the use of force in the region will “diminish the closer we get to the elections” — and that Iran likely understands that dynamic.

“They think we’ve got a short attention span, we’re obsessed by the elections,” Bolton said.

When Vargas noted the president recently said he doesn’t care about the midterms, Bolton pushed back, saying, “That’s all he cares about.”

“This is about price at the pump of gasoline,” he continued. “And he thinks if he can get the Strait of Hormuz open, global price of petroleum will fall, price at the pump here will fall, inflation will fall and election chances will look better in November. That’s exactly what it’s about.”

data.title: Live updates: Trump considers Iran deal; Bondi faces House Oversight questions on Epstein files
data.description:
President Trump said Friday morning that he was heading to the White House Situation Room to make a “final determination” on a tentative deal in the Iran conflict.

Trump, in a Truth Social post, laid out the situation he was considering, saying no money would change hands, the Strait of Hormuz would open and the U.S. blockade would end.

Former Attorney General Pam Bondi appeared before the House Oversight and Government Reform Committee for an interview Friday morning about the Justice Department’s release of the Jeffrey Epstein files.

Bondi refused to answer questions about her conversations with Trump about the Epstein files and repeatedly threw Acting Attorney General Todd Blanche under the bus, according to Democrats.

Several musical acts have pulled out of performances for the Freedom 250 “Great American State Fair,” citing complaints that they were misled. A Freedom 250 spokesperson says the event is “nonpolitical.” Read more about it here.

Meanwhile, reports that the Justice Department will investigate the funding sources made available for writer E. Jean Carroll to sue Trump for defamation is sparking a backlash on both sides of the aisle.

Follow live below.

data.title: US sanctions just targeted Hezbollah's real shield — the Lebanese state itself
data.description:
The U.S. Treasury didn’t sanction a militia last week. It sanctioned a system.

Nine people were designated last Thursday — Hezbollah parliamentarians Hassan Fadlallah, Ibrahim al-Mousawi, Hussein al-Hajj Hassan, and Mohammed Fneish, but also Amal Movement security figures Ahmad Baalbaki and Ahmad Safawi, and an Iranian diplomat accused of coordinating Hezbollah’s regional support networks.

That list is not an escalation in degree. It is an escalation in kind.

But what is most significant is the designation of Colonel Samer Hamadi, who heads Lebanese Army Intelligence in the southern suburbs of Beirut — Hezbollah’s urban stronghold — and Brigadier General Khattar Nasser al-Din, who directs the Analysis Department at Lebanese General Security, the agency that controls passports and border movement. Neither commands combat troops. Both commanded information.

The Lebanese Army’s prompt public statement that its soldiers remain “loyal” following the Hamadi designation is itself revealing — an institution that felt no need to respond has suddenly felt compelled to. That defensive reflex is more telling against its documented backdrop: Washington had already canceled the Lebanese Armed Forces commander’s meetings over Lebanon’s failure to act on Hezbollah’s weapons — a diplomatic rebuke that preceded Thursday’s sanctions by months. The loyalty statement that followed is not institutional confidence. It is institutional cover.

For years, Washington sanctioned Hezbollah as though the group were a militia operating at Lebanon’s margins. It is not. Hezbollah survives because the Lebanese state — parts of its parliament, its security services, its administrative apparatus — has sheltered it, laundered its influence, and normalized its parallel authority. This is the architecture Qassem Soleimani built: militia fused with mafia, weapons protected by patronage, coercion normalized through institutional complicity.

Sanctioning Hezbollah members of parliament is not new. Sanctioning Lebanese Army Intelligence officers is. That distinction matters enormously. The Lebanese Armed Forces have long been treated as a red line by Washington — the one institution worth protecting, the thin reed of legitimate state authority in a hollowed-out republic. Designating Hamadi and Nasser al-Din signals that this protection is now conditional. State uniforms no longer confer immunity if the person wearing them serves the militia’s security architecture.

Equally significant is the Amal dimension. Washington has historically treated Amal and Hezbollah as distinct — politically useful fiction that allowed it to engage Speaker Nabih Berri while sanctioning the group he coordinates with. Targeting Amal-linked security officials ends that fiction. It reflects a more accurate understanding of how Lebanese power actually works: not as separate sectarian blocs, but as an interlocking ecosystem of influence, coercion, and shared economic interest.

One angle deserves more attention: the sanctions already target institutions beyond Hezbollah’s own apparatus. Amal Movement security officials, Lebanese General Security leadership, and Army Intelligence — these are not Hezbollah. They are the broader ecosystem that makes Hezbollah’s parallel state function. The country’s collapse was not caused by one militia. It was enabled by a system of patronage, coercion, and institutional complicity that crosses organizational lines.

The timing is deliberate. These sanctions land as Lebanon-Israel security talks approach — a military meeting set for May 29, and political negotiations in Washington on June 2-3. The message to any Lebanese actor contemplating obstruction is plain: the cost of protecting Hezbollah’s privileges is no longer zero.

But no one should mistake pressure for transformation. Hezbollah’s system is adaptive by design. It will reroute funds, protect loyalists, and wait for Western attention to move elsewhere — as it has done successfully for decades. Hezbollah has already dismissed the sanctions as having “absolutely no effect.” That is partly bluster. It is also partly true.

Sanctions alone will not disarm Hezbollah or restore Lebanese sovereignty. What they can do — if sustained — is raise the cost of complicity. They can strip away the fiction that state officials who enable Hezbollah are somehow separate from it. They can give reformers inside Lebanon a stronger argument: that institutional survival now requires institutional independence.

According to Al-Arabiya’s senior Washington reporter, more than 100 additional names are under review — a figure that, if accurate, would signal a sustained campaign rather than a one-time gesture. If Washington follows through — expanding designations to judicial, financial and administrative collaborators — it will have constructed something more durable than a list. It will have built a sustained accountability architecture.

The question is whether it will. The United States has a documented history of applying pressure at Lebanese inflection points, then allowing it to dissipate when regional attention shifts. Hezbollah’s system is engineered to outlast that pattern. It has before.

These sanctions are the most structurally significant U.S. action against Hezbollah’s ecosystem in years. Whether they become a turning point or another chapter in a long history of insufficient pressure depends on one thing: whether Washington stays the course long enough for the cost of complicity to actually change Lebanese political calculations.

That is not guaranteed. But for the first time in a long time, it is possible.

Charbel A. Antoun is a Washington-based journalist and writer specializing in U.S. foreign policy, with a focus on the Middle East and North Africa.

data.title: JFK Airport to screen airline passengers from Ebola outbreak nations
data.description:
John F. Kennedy International Airport in New York City will now begin enhanced Ebola screenings for travelers arriving from countries affected by the ongoing outbreak in Central and East Africa, the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) announced on Thursday.

Beginning at 11:59 PM on Thursday, JFK has been added to the list of designated airports in the U.S. conducting public health screenings for passengers who recently traveled to the Democratic Republic of Congo, Uganda or South Sudan.

Other ports of entry that were already operating screening programs include Washington Dulles International Airport, Hartsfield-Jackson Atlanta International Airport and Houston’s George Bush Intercontinental Airport.

“Public health entry screening serves as one component of CDC’s layered public health approach, which also includes overseas exit screening, airline illness reporting, and post-arrival public health monitoring,” the CDC said in a statement on the social platform X.

Under the screening program, travelers who have been to Congo, Uganda or South Sudan within the past 21 days must enter the country through those four airports with designated screening areas upon arrival.

They will answer health-related questions and disclose any potential exposure risks. Health officials will also conduct temperature checks and check passengers for any symptoms.

Federal officials implemented the Ebola screening procedures earlier this month in response to the outbreak caused by the Bundibugyo virus in Congo and neighboring Uganda, which has led to more than 300 suspected cases and 88 deaths. There is no vaccine vor the virus.

The World Health Organization declared it a public health emergency of international concern on May 17, and the CDC temporarily blocked non-U.S. citizens and lawful permanent residents who recently traveled to the affected region from entering the country.

Ahead of the anticipated FIFA World Cup hosted in North America this summer, the U.S., Mexico and Canada released a joint statement on Thursday announcing planned public health travel measures for individuals traveling from the regions in Africa that are at greatest risk from Ebola.

data.title: Louisiana legislature passes new GOP-favored map after Supreme Court ruling
data.description:
The Louisiana legislature passed a new GOP-favored House map on Friday that eliminates one of the state’s Black-majority congressional districts after the U.S. Supreme Court ruled the Bayou State’s map unconstitutional earlier this month. 

The Louisiana Senate had originally passed new congressional lines earlier this month in a 27-10 vote, but the state House had added amendments to the legislation earlier this week that sent the map back to the state Senate for approval.  

The amendments were approved on a 28-10 vote. It now heads to Gov. Jeff Landry’s (R) desk for his signature. He’s expected to swiftly sign the new House map into law.  

DEVELOPING…

data.title: Drag queen Pattie Gonia sounds off on trademark battle with Patagonia
data.description:
PORTLAND, Ore. (KOIN) — A drag queen is speaking out against an outdoor clothing company’s lawsuit against her.

In a video shared on Instagram on Wednesday, Pattie Gonia condemned Patagonia’s decision to accuse her of trademark infringement in a federal complaint filed earlier in January. The lawsuit alleged the Bend-based climate activist’s trademark application for her name “directly” competes with the apparel and gear brand’s own products and services.

The complaint states plaintiffs are seeking just $1 in damages, but according to Pattie Gonia — who is also known as Wyn Wiley — the company has threatened to strip her of her drag persona and cost her more than $1 million in legal fees.

“Isn’t it interesting that the multi-billion-dollar corporation Patagonia chose this exact moment, the height of anti-LGBTQ+ politics and attacks on the environment to sue me,” Pattie Gonia said, in part, in her video statement. “They’ve known about me for eight years and the impact of this timing right now is serious and it’s real. I think they looked at the political moment and thought they could pull this off without much pushback.”

Patagonia has attempted to reach a mutual agreement with her over the years before taking legal action, according to a statement from the company. The plaintiff’s attorneys cited a 2022 agreement in which Pattie Gonia ensured her partnership with Hydroflask would not use fonts or designs that resembled the brand’s — but the drag queen has argued those terms applied to that particular partnership, not her persona as a whole.

She further noted that a South American region also known as Patagonia predates both her and the 1973-founded corporation by hundreds of years.

Yet the apparel brand alleged the performer’s merchandise has already caused confusion among customers, and will continue to do so.

“Patagonia has a responsibility to protect the company that generations of employees have helped build,” a Patagonia spokesperson said, in part, in a statement. “Not because a name matters more than people, but because that name carries trust, purpose, and decades of work connected to environmental activism, product, storytelling and community impact. Protecting the Patagonia trademark is part of protecting the ability of this company to continue doing that work in the future.”

The company added that it is not pursuing financial gain through the lawsuit that Pattie Gonia is pushing to have dropped. The drag queen urged fans not to target the brand’s employees over the lawsuit, and also claimed her recent statement is “just part one” of the story involving the corporation.

data.title: What lies ahead for the Republican Party after Trump?
data.description:
On Jan. 20, 2029, President Trump’s second term will end. So, what’s next for the nation and the Republican Party? The challenge for Republicans will be to rearticulate the principles and priorities that could drive their party for a generation. This is important because political parties are the vehicle that our system uses to hold government accountable for its actions.

The Republican Party’s future is not preordained. Trumpism could prevail with a new leader. Better yet, principled pragmatic conservatism could reemerge and reclaim the party’s future, or a hybrid coalition that blends populism with legacy Republicanism could be born.

New and creative leadership will be needed if the party is to move beyond the shadow of Trump’s dominance of the party. This will not be an easy task, given the schisms that currently exist in the party over core issues, such as managing the economy, America’s role in foreign affairs, and the many Trump loyalists seeing politics primarily through the lens of cultural conflicts.

Like all broad-based political parties, the Republican Party’s future will be molded by competing factions, each holding differing ideological and strategic visions. The Republican Party’s internal debates are not new, but Trump has magnified them. Today, there are three major factions that will play key roles in defining the party’s future.

First, there are the Trump loyalists who center on his nationalist, anti-establishment and protectionist agenda. MAGA populists prioritize immigration crackdowns, cultural grievances, long-term populist realignment, protectionism, state capitalism, and a more interventionist federal government.

Then there are the legacy Republicans who stands for fiscal responsibility, constitutional due process and the rule of law, foreign policy built on strong alliances to support democracies, and free enterprise. Groups, such as the one where I serve as a state chair and leader, campaign at the grassroots level to restore these core conservative principles as the party’s North Star.

Finally, there are Christian Nationalists, ideological think tanks such as the Heritage Foundation, and new right splinter groups skeptical of traditional Republican positions that will also want their voices heard.

While the post-Trump Republican Party will be shaped by these distinct factions, each pulling the party in different directions, the central struggle is likely between MAGA and the legacy wing of the party.

Given recent history, which faction will prevail?

Although Trump’s administration stressed economic populism, cultural grievance and institutional distrust, it did not establish a governing philosophy. This enabled various MAGA supporters to focus on their priorities, and not necessarily the movement’s.

According to More in Common’s extensive research, less than 40 percent of Trump voters say being MAGA is important to their political identity. This suggests that the movement is centered around Trump’s personality more than a comprehensive governing vision. Therefore, traditional conservative legacy Republicans have an opportunity to influence the direction of the post-Trump Republican Party. However, this will require that they are well-financed and organized to pursue their goals, and can attract working-class Trump voters.

The potential to restore conservative principles and regain the party’s future will also depend on what transpires across the political aisle. If Democrats move too far to the left, moderates and independents may be looking for a more centrist and pragmatic political home which could benefit traditional Republicans.

William Galston, writing in the Wall Street Journal, observed that “when Bernie Sanders’s left-wing insurgency narrowly failed in 2016, most of his followers remained within the party.” After former President Joe Biden’s shortcomings became apparent, other leftists joined the Democratic Socialist movement and used it as a base to strengthen the progressive wing of the Democratic Party. Zohran Mamdani’s mayoral victory in New York City is triggering like-minded candidacies in Democratic primaries throughout the nation.

Nobody can predict with certainty what the country’s political landscape will be in the future, but it could be very different when Trump departs the White House.

Gary Sasse is the Rhode Island chair of Our Republican Legacy and served as director Rhode Island’s Department of Administration.

data.title: Speaker Johnson's longtime top aide departing office
data.description:
Speaker Mike Johnson’s (R-La.) chief of staff, Hayden Haynes — one of his longest-serving aides — will depart the office in early June. 

Haynes started as campaign manager for Johnson when he first ran for Congress in 2016, becoming his chief of staff once Johnson took office. He stayed in the role as Johnson’s top aide through his stunning and unlikely rise to the Speakership after former Rep. Kevin McCarthy (R-Calif.) was ousted from the role in 2023.

Punchbowl News first reported Haynes’s coming departure. Johnson’s office did not say what Haynes is doing next.

Garrett Fultz, Johnson’s current deputy chief of staff, will rise to replace Haynes as chief of staff.

There will also be other staff promotions in the Speaker’s office. 

Johnson’s floor director Chris Bien, member services director Courtney Butcher, and policy director Bill Duhnke will become deputy chiefs. 

As the Speaker’s chief of staff, Haynes oversaw a number of contentious legislative fights and helped steer President Trump’s One Big Beautiful Bill Act of tax cuts and other priorities through the House.

Haynes was also the subject of controversy last year when he was arrested for DUI after allegedly backing into a parked vehicle after Trump’s address. He pleaded not guilty, and Johnson stood by his longtime aide.

data.title: Cornyn shares fable of frog and scorpion after Trump sinks re-election
data.description:
Sen. John Cornyn (R-Texas) cryptically shared the fable of the frog and the scorpion on social media after President Trump torpedoed his re-election campaign by endorsing state Attorney General Ken Paxton, who cruised to victory in the Texas Senate GOP primary Tuesday.

Cornyn, who touted his record of voting with Trump 99.3 percent of the time, posted the famous fable about betrayal on the social media platform X, but left it ambiguous what the coded message was intended to say.

“An old but apt fable,” Cornyn wrote on Friday morning above the story of a scorpion who wants to cross a river but cannot swim.

Cornyn recounts how the scorpion asks the frog for a ride to the other side but the frog hesitates, worried that it might get stung.

The scorpion points out to the reluctant frog that it would also drown if it stung and killed the frog in the middle of the river, convincing it to agree to the proposition.

“Midway across the river, the scorpion stings the frog anyway, dooming them both. The dying frog asks the scorpion why it stung despite knowing the consequence, to which the scorpion replies: ‘I am sorry, but I couldn’t help myself. It’s my character,’” Cornyn posted.

The post didn’t include any additional commentary and Cornyn’s office didn’t comment on what the senator was trying to convey by sharing the parable.

But the story raises questions about what Cornyn might be thinking about Trump’s decision to endorse Paxton, whom Senate Republican leaders viewed as a much weaker candidate than Cornyn to defend the Texas Senate seat in the general election.

Trump on Sunday blasted Cornyn on Truth Social as “VERY disloyal to me” because he “didn’t fight hard enough for the desperately needed SAVE AMERICA ACT.”

That sharp criticism might not be sitting well with Cornyn who flipped his position on preserving the Senate filibuster in March to call in a New York Post-published op-ed on Senate Republicans to change the rules to pass the SAVE America Act.

“After careful consideration, I support whatever changes to Senate rules that may prove necessary for us to get the SAVE America Act and homeland security funding past the Democrats’ obstruction, through the Senate, and on the president’s desk for his signature,” Cornyn wrote in The Post, breaking from the pledge he made during the 2024 Senate GOP leadership race to preserve the 60-vote threshold for passing legislation if elected Senate Majority Leader.

Cornyn also served as Senate Republican whip during the first two years of Trump’s first term in the White House and played a key role in helping to pass the 2017 Tax Cuts and Jobs Act, one of Trump’s signature legislative achievements

Cornyn noted that he had a stronger pro-Trump voting record than 95 out of 100 senators during Trump’s first term and that he endorsed Trump in 2024 with his earliest-ever endorsement in a contested GOP presidential primary.

That still wasn’t enough to earn Trump’s endorsement in the Texas Senate primary, even though Senate Majority Leader John Thune (R-S.D.) and other Senate Republicans lobbied Trump for months to back Cornyn.

Senate Republican strategists had circulated an estimate before Tuesday’s primary that it would cost the party more than $200 million to help Paxton beat Democratic candidate James Talarico in the general election.
